entities.description: Public electronic text encoding the 1901 edition title page and main medieval texts on printed pp. 1–136. It omits the preface, introduction, notes, and glossary, although a terminal PB points to p. 137.

rights.basis: The current U-M item search record applies No Copyright – United States; the institutional site terms still apply to service use.

bibliographic.citation: George H. McKnight, ed., King Horn, Floriz and Blauncheflur, The Assumption of Our Lady, first edited by J. Rawson Lumby, EETS Original Series 14 (London: Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co. for the Early English Text Society, 1901).
bibliographic.publisher: Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co. for the Early English Text Society
bibliographic.series: Early English Text Society, Original Series 14
bibliographic.title: King Horn, Floriz and Blauncheflur, The Assumption of Our Lady

entities.description: The direct printed source of CME00050. McKnight re-edited Lumby's 1866 EETS collection from the manuscripts, with introduction, notes, and glossary. The volume presents three independent works and does not provide complete textual material for every surviving Floris or Assumption witness.
entities.label: McKnight's 1901 re-edition of King Horn, Floriz and Blauncheflur, and The Assumption of Our Lady

physicalDescription.extent: lviii preliminary pages and 171 numbered pages
notes: Contents: preface and introduction pp. v–lviii; King Horn pp. 1–69; Floris pp. 71–110; Assumption pp. 111–136; notes pp. 137–154; glossary pp. 155–171.

rights.basis: The edition was published in 1901 and is public domain in the United States; this does not determine rights in later scan files or provider packaging.
rights.component: 1901 printed edition text and editorial matter

entities.description: The first publication of the three-work EETS collection. Lumby prints CUL Gg.4.27(2) for all three works, Cotton Vitellius D III for Floris, and Additional 10036 for the Assumption; only short Laud and Harley King Horn specimens appear in the preface.

dateStatements.label: Manuscript production
dateStatements.value: First quarter of the fourteenth century

entities.description: A separate bound fourteen-leaf parchment quire containing Floris and Blancheflour on ff. 1r–5v, King Horn on ff. 6r–13r, and the opening 240 lines of the Assumption on ff. 13v–14v. The manuscript is incomplete at both ends; no other part of its former larger codex is known to survive.
entities.label: Cambridge University Library, MS Gg.4.27(2)

physicalDescription.extent: i + 14 + i leaves; one quire, separately bound
notes: The same Anglicana hand writes all three works; the current binding dates to 1983.

Identify King Horn separately as provisional target ff. 83r–92v, and confirm that mapping against the supplied foliation before associating image files with the work.
The service may quote large, fragile, or difficult orders separately; reproduction or publication of supplied images requires written permission under the imaging terms.

rights.basis: The imaging terms state that supplied material may be protected and may not be reproduced, including online or in publications, without written permission; applicable fees and third-party rights are item-specific.

entities.description: A twenty-six-leaf parchment codex damaged in the 1731 Cotton Library fire. The badly burnt and shrunken Floris and Blancheflour fragments occupy ff. 6r–8v.
entities.label: British Library, Cotton MS Vitellius D III

notes: The Floris component is ff. 6–8; the codex was rebound in 1970.

rights.basis: The imaging terms require written permission before reproducing supplied material and leave copyright and fees item-specific.

Identify the McKnight continuation separately as provisional target ff. 78v–86r within the parent record's Assumption envelope ff. 75r–86r, but explicitly flag the child record's conflicting ff. 124–126 claim and require the Library to confirm current foliation before associating image files with the work.
The service may quote large, fragile, or difficult orders separately; reproduction or publication of supplied images requires written permission under the imaging terms.

rights.basis: The imaging terms require written permission before reproducing supplied material and leave copyright and fees item-specific.

entities.description: A late-fifteenth-century paper manuscript. The parent catalogue contents place The Assumption of Our Lady at ff. 75r–86r; McKnight uses the continuation corresponding to lines 241–715, supported by edition milestones as ff. 78v–86r. A component child record instead says ff. 124–126 and is retained as a conflict, not as the work locator.

holding.notes: The parent record is controlling for the present whole-object description; its exact contents and McKnight's milestones outweigh the contradictory child locus pending BL clarification.

entities.label: British Library, Harley MS 2382
notes: Do not use the child record's ff. 124–126 claim for work mapping unless the British Library reconciles it against the parent contents and supplied images.

physicalDescription.extent: 129 leaves plus flyleaves according to the parent record

rights.basis: The imaging terms require written permission before reproducing supplied material and leave copyright and fees item-specific.

notes: McKnight describes a small volume of about one hundred folios; the current catalog record should control any whole-object order extent.

access.accessMethod: British Library digitisation service and online order form
access.entityLabel: British Library digitisation and imaging service
access.format: Service information, order form, terms, and PDF rate card

localCopies.label: Complete British Library digitisation rate card 2025/26

workPortion.label: Rates relevant to whole-manuscript photography requests
locators: scanning and photographic reproduction price tables

notes: The 2025/26 card lists standard scan JPEG charges of £15.25 for the first page and £0.58 for each subsequent page, photographic TIFF charges of £27.70 for the first image and £7.25 for each subsequent image, and complex photography at £58.19 per image.

Manuscripts normally require photography rather than ordinary scanning unless an acceptable surrogate exists; specify color and both recto and verso. Published rates are indicative because complete codices and fragile or difficult material may be quoted outside standard pricing.

entities.description: Current service, order form, terms, and 2025/26 rate card for requesting manuscript photography. Manuscript orders may require quotation and special handling; supplied images remain subject to written reuse permissions and any rights identified by the Library.
entities.label: British Library digitisation and imaging service

next_steps: Submit one exact whole-codex request per shelfmark, explicitly including covers or boards, spine, pastedowns, all flyleaves, every recto and verso including blanks, adjacent works, binding fragments, and edges where safe.
Require the Library to identify any omitted or unphotographable surfaces, return current foliation metadata, quote the entire order, and state repository and publication permissions in writing.
Cache each supplied complete object under source-cache/CME00050 and only then add exact local_copies[].work_portion mappings.
openQuestions.question: Can complete present-object color photography be obtained for all five British Library codices, and what surfaces, handling limits, total costs, and reuse permissions will apply?

next_steps: Ask the British Library for a catalog correction or explanation before final mapping.
Use the requested whole-codex images to verify current foliation and map McKnight's line 241 beginning and terminal leaf directly.
openQuestions.question: How should the British Library reconcile Harley 2382's parent contents and McKnight/PCMEP evidence ending at f. 86r with the component child record's ff. 124–126 claim?

next_steps: Preserve both attributed date claims and consult recent codicological work before normalizing a narrower witness date.
openQuestions.question: How should the current British Library fourteenth-century date for the Cotton Floris component be reconciled with scholarly witness dates near the late thirteenth century?

next_steps: Compare typographic accidents across both impressions or request U-M production documentation; retain McKnight 1901 as the textual edition layer either way.
openQuestions.question: Was the U-M item keyed directly from a 1901 impression or from the 1962 reprint of the 1901 text?

assertion.notes: The identifiers and repository provenance support this direction, but the bulk import does not document transfer mechanics or byte identity with a U-M export.

reviewNotes: The collection, its three independent works, the nine XML witness segments, the eight logical edited streams, the seven physical manuscripts, and the printed and digital carriers are distinct units.
Complete manuscript-image coverage means the complete provider-published canvas inventory, not a claim that unexposed spine, edge, or preservation-master views exist.

notes: The 2025/26 card lists standard scan JPEG charges of £15.25 for the first page and £0.58 for each subsequent page, photographic TIFF charges of £27.70 for the first image and £7.25 for each subsequent image, and complex photography at £58.19 per image.

Manuscripts normally require photography rather than ordinary scanning unless an acceptable surrogate exists; specify color and both recto and verso. Published rates are indicative because complete codices and fragile or difficult material may be quoted outside standard pricing.

lineage.summary: CME00050 is the repository XML representation of the title page and printed pp. 1–136 of George H. McKnight's 1901 re-edition of EETS OS 14, a collection of three independent Middle English works first edited by J. Rawson Lumby in 1866. McKnight presents King Horn from three manuscripts, Floris and Blancheflour from three manuscripts, and The Assumption of the Virgin in a composite Cambridge-plus-Harley stream beside Additional 10036. Complete provider-published whole-object image bundles are cached for CUL Gg.4.27(2) and Bodleian Laud Misc. 108 with exact work mappings. No public whole-object image download was found for the five British Library codices; their request routes specify the entire present physical object and require foliation confirmation before final work mapping.

catalogSummary.author: Three anonymous medieval poets or translators; no shared authorship
catalogSummary.date: Works composed c. 1200–1290; collection first published 1866 and re-edited 1901
catalogSummary.editor: George H. McKnight, revising Joseph Rawson Lumby
catalogSummary.region: England; proposed origins vary by work from southern and southeastern England to the East Midlands
catalogSummary.title: King Horn; Floriz and Blauncheflur; The Assumption of Our Lady

catalogingSubject.label: King Horn; Floriz and Blauncheflur; The Assumption of Our Lady
catalogingSubject.scope_note: George H. McKnight's 1901 EETS re-edition of three independent anonymous Middle English verse works, represented in CME00050 by the edition title page and printed pp. 1–136. The collection, its three works, their parallel edited witness streams, the manuscripts, the printed editions, and the digital encodings are distinct cataloging layers.

contentParts.label: King Horn
contentParts.summary: Anonymous Middle English romance presented in three complete parallel witness versions.

contentParts.label: King Horn: Cambridge version
contentParts.summary: The version from CUL MS Gg.4.27(2), currently fols. 6r–13r.

contentParts.label: King Horn: Laud version
contentParts.summary: The version from Bodleian Library MS Laud Misc. 108, fols. 219va–228rb.

contentParts.label: King Horn: Harley version
notes: The 824 XML L elements are encoding units, not a claim that the Harley poem has only 824 metrical lines.
contentParts.summary: The version from British Library Harley MS 2253, fols. 83r–92v; XML line elements often contain two metrical lines separated by a bar.

contentParts.label: Floriz and Blauncheflur
contentParts.summary: Anonymous Middle English romance adapted from French, presented from three of four surviving witnesses; all surviving copies lack the beginning.

contentParts.label: Floris and Blancheflour: Egerton version
notes: McKnight describes the manuscript text as 1,083 lines; 1,140 is the repository's XML L-element count.
contentParts.summary: The principal version from British Library Egerton MS 2862, fols. 98r–111r, called the Trentham manuscript in the edition.

contentParts.label: Floris and Blancheflour: Cotton fragments
notes: McKnight counts 451 preserved lines but only about 180 as fully legible; 453 is the XML L-element count.
contentParts.summary: Fire-damaged fragments from British Library Cotton MS Vitellius D III, fols. 6r–8v, printed in four non-contiguous blocks.

contentParts.label: Floris and Blancheflour: Cambridge fragment
notes: McKnight counts 824 lines; 852 is the XML L-element count.
contentParts.summary: The fragment from CUL MS Gg.4.27(2), fols. 1r–5v.

contentParts.label: The Assumption of Our Lady
contentParts.summary: Anonymous Marian legend in two parallel edited streams: a Cambridge fragment continued from Harley 2382, and the fuller Additional 10036 text.

contentParts.label: Assumption: Cambridge fragment with Harley continuation
contentParts.summary: One continuous left-hand edition stream assembled from two different manuscripts; represented as two DIV2 segments in the XML.

contentParts.label: Assumption: Cambridge fragment
contentParts.summary: The opening 240-line fragment from CUL MS Gg.4.27(2), fols. 13v–14v.

contentParts.label: Assumption: Harley continuation
notes: The BL component child record's incompatible 'ff. 124–6' claim is preserved as an open question rather than used as this locator.
contentParts.summary: Continuation corresponding to lines 241–715 from Harley MS 2382, fols. 78v–86r according to McKnight's milestones and the current parent catalogue contents.

contentParts.label: Assumption: Additional 10036 version
notes: McKnight counts 904 lines; 915 is the XML L-element count.
contentParts.summary: The fuller parallel version from British Library Additional MS 10036, fols. 62r–80r in McKnight's edition milestones.

scope.description: Scholarly composition range for King Horn, not the dates of its surviving manuscripts
Scholarly composition range for the Middle English adaptation
Scholarly composition range for The Assumption of the Virgin
Lumby's first EETS publication of the three-work collection
McKnight's re-edition, the direct printed source of CME00050

extent.description: Representation metrics for the repository XML: three top-level works, nine DIV2 witness segments, fourteen LG elements, and 8,220 L elements. These counts include parallel versions and must not be summed as the authored length of any one work. The file has 353 PB milestones and 138 distinct image REF values, with the edition title page and printed pp. 1–136 represented; one terminal PB points to p. 137.

formStatements.description: All three medieval works are rhymed verse. The prose editorial apparatus is a publication layer, not a change in the works' textual form.

scope.description: The three medieval verse works and all nine encoded witness streams
McKnight's title matter, introduction, headings, notes, glossary, and editorial apparatus; CME00050 encodes only the title and main texts
Brief manuscript rubrics, explicits, and editorial labels within the edition

notes: The preferred title uses semicolons to make the three-work collection boundary human-readable; the edition title page uses commas.
The Auchinleck Floris witness is part of the work's surviving tradition but is not an upstream source for McKnight's printed Floris streams and is therefore not bound as a source witness here.
XML line counts are representation metrics. McKnight's scholarly line counts differ where lines are combined, split, supplied, or damaged.
The repository encoding omits McKnight's preface, introduction, notes, and glossary, despite retaining a terminal PB reference to printed p. 137.

next_steps: Ask the British Library to reconcile the parent and child records before treating any requested image sequence as mapped.
When a whole-codex facsimile is supplied, confirm the current foliation directly and then update the work portion.
openQuestions.question: Why does the British Library Harley 2382 component child record give ff. 124–126 for the Assumption when the parent contents, McKnight's edition, PCMEP, and the XML's internal leaf milestone support the section ending at f. 86r?

next_steps: Preserve both attributed dating claims and seek recent codicological literature before normalizing a witness date.
openQuestions.question: Should Cotton Vitellius D III's Floris fragment be cataloged by the current British Library component date of the fourteenth century or by scholarly text-witness dates near the late thirteenth century?

next_steps: Compare typographic accidents and pagination against both impressions or request U-M production documentation.
openQuestions.question: Was CME00050 keyed from an original 1901 impression or from the 1962 Oxford reprint of the 1901 text?

summaries.value: Three independent Middle English verse works: King Horn in Cambridge, Laud, and Harley versions; Floris and Blancheflour in Egerton, Cotton, and Cambridge versions; and The Assumption of the Virgin as a composite Cambridge-plus-Harley stream beside a complete Additional 10036 stream.

The nine XML DIV2 units are edited witness streams, not nine separately authored works. The Cambridge and Harley Assumption fragments form one continuous left-hand edition stream even though the XML models them as two divisions.
